context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. | Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Informal Economy and Illegality

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Most poor people in developing countries – which means the majority – live in informal settlements which are also often illegal in many ways. How does informalization happen? What is the relationship between the informal and formal economy? Who plans for outcomes in informal economies? Is formalization — of tenure arrangements etc. — the answer?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, I’m kinda lost on this informal economy reflection tbh. idk how to even start or what the prompt is really asking.
exchanges.tutor: No worries, let's break it down together. Can you re-read the prompt and tell me: how many distinct questions is it actually asking you to address?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very beginning and feeling lost. I should help them break down the prompt without giving away any content or direction. The best first move is to ask them to read the prompt carefully and identify what questions it's actually asking, rather than me summarizing it for them. This activates their own reading comprehension and gives them ownership of the starting point.

exchanges.student: uh maybe like 2? or 4? idk bc they all blur together for me rn.
exchanges.tutor: Try listing them out one by one — just copy the questions from the prompt and number them. How many do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is unsure about the number of questions in the prompt. Rather than telling them the answer, I should push them to look more carefully at the prompt itself. Counting the questions is a simple but important first step that will help them structure their reflection piece. I'll ask them to list them out explicitly.

exchanges.student: i think it’s like: how informalization happens, how informal/formal are related, who plans stuff, and if formalization is the answer. so 4 i guess, but idk what any of that really means.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right, four questions. Good. Let's start with the first one — how does informalization happen? Before looking at any readings, what's your gut instinct: why might someone end up living in an informal settlement rather than formal housing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified the four questions. I should affirm this and then use guided discovery to get them thinking about the content. Rather than explaining what the questions mean, I should pick the first one and ask them to think about it from their own knowledge/intuition before diving into readings. This keeps the Socratic approach and gets them generating their own ideas.

exchanges.student: tbh my brain just goes to “they’re poor” and then I get stuck. like idk if that’s too simple or wrong.
exchanges.tutor: That's a reasonable starting point, not wrong. But push it a little further — if someone is poor in a city in a developing country, what specifically might they be unable to access that would push them toward informal housing?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a basic intuition that is partially correct but incomplete. Rather than validating or dismissing it, I should push them to think deeper by asking what being poor actually means in this context - what does it prevent people from accessing? This will help them move from 'they're poor' to a more structural understanding of informalization without me giving the answer.

exchanges.student: idk like jobs, legal land, permits, services maybe? ngl it all feels super broad and mushy.
exchanges.tutor: Those are actually solid observations. Now here's a connecting question: if legal land, permits, and services are all things the *formal* system controls — what does that tell you about the relationship between the formal and informal economies?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is generating relevant ideas but feeling overwhelmed by their breadth. I should affirm the direction and help them see that 'broad and mushy' is actually a sign they're touching on something structural. I'll push them to think about what connects these things - the relationship between poverty and the formal system's barriers - which will help them transition naturally to the second question about informal/formal relationships.

exchanges.student: maybe that informal stuff exists bc formal systems don’t include everyone? but then idk if informal is separate from formal or kinda depends on it.
exchanges.tutor: That tension you just identified — separate vs. dependent — is actually really important. Can you think of a concrete example where informal economic activity might actually *rely* on the formal economy in some way?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a genuinely insightful observation about the relationship between formal and informal economies - this is actually quite good thinking. I should affirm this and push them to develop it further, particularly the second part of their thought about dependence vs. separation, which is a key tension in the literature. This keeps momentum going without giving away content.

exchanges.student: maybe street vendors selling stuff made by formal companies? or workers doing informal jobs for formal businesses? i’m not sure if that’s the kind of example u mean.
exchanges.tutor: Both examples work well — you're essentially describing how the two sectors are intertwined, not separate. Good. Now flip to the planning question: if informal settlements aren't really planned by governments, who *do* you think shapes how they develop and function?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is generating good concrete examples that illustrate the interdependence between formal and informal economies. I should affirm this and move them toward the third and fourth questions, since we've spent enough time on the first two and have about 4 exchanges left in the planned conversation. I want to push them toward thinking about planning and formalization now.

exchanges.student: maybe residents themselves, landlords, local bosses, NGOs, politicians? tbh this is where I kinda want the big picture explained bc I can’t tell what argument I’m supposed to make.
exchanges.tutor: Your list is actually strong. But here's the thing — this is a *reflection* piece, meaning the argument is yours to make, not one to be discovered. So let me push you to the last question, which is really where your argument lives: do you think formalizing tenure and legal status actually solves the problems you've been describing? What's your instinct?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is generating good answers but is now asking me to explain the 'argument they're supposed to make' - this is a boundary moment. I need to clarify that a reflection piece requires their own argument, not a pre-determined one. I should also affirm their list and nudge them toward the last and most argumentative question - formalization - which is where they'll need to take a real stance. With about 3 exchanges left, I should start pushing them toward synthesis.

exchanges.student: my instinct is “not fully,” but idk why exactly, and I feel like I’m missing the whole picture fr.
exchanges.tutor: Look back at what you already said: informal settlements exist because formal systems exclude people, the two economies are intertwined, and residents/local actors shape informal spaces. Given all that — why might simply giving someone a land title *not* be enough to change their situation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a valid instinct but can't articulate why. With only a couple exchanges left, I should help them connect their instinct to the ideas they've already generated in this conversation rather than introducing new concepts. They've already built the reasoning - they just haven't connected the dots yet. I'll point them back to what they said earlier.

exchanges.student: maybe bc a title doesn’t automatically give jobs, infrastructure, safety, or political power? but idk, can u just explain the overall picture bc I still feel super lost.
exchanges.tutor: I'm not going to explain it for you — but look at what you just said: a title doesn't give jobs, infrastructure, safety, or political power. That *is* the big picture argument you were looking for. You've actually built a coherent response across this whole conversation. It's time to start drafting — take everything you've said here and put it into your own words on paper. What's your first sentence going to be?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the overall picture, which I should refuse to do. However, they have actually just made a very strong argument on their own - their last message contains the core of a solid reflection. I should point this out clearly and push them to start writing rather than continuing to discuss, since we're at the end of the planned conversation length and they've built enough to draft something. This is the right moment to push for synthesis.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout the conversation, consistently drawing ideas out of the student rather than providing them. All four prompt questions were addressed through guided questioning, the student generated the key arguments independently, and the tutor explicitly refused to give the 'big picture' explanation when asked, redirecting the student to their own prior reasoning. No significant deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: Under 1.1, the tutor never gave submission-ready content, never rewrote student text, and never stated conclusions before the student reasoned toward them — the student independently arrived at all key ideas (exclusion, interdependence, limits of formalization). Under 1.2, the tutor consistently built on each student response and used diagnostic questioning before advancing. Under 1.3, the tutor provided process-level guidance (e.g., 'this is a reflection piece, the argument is yours to make'). Dialogue quality was strong: no repetition without progress, no assignment drift, and the tutor kept the conversation anchored to the four prompt questions throughout. Communication was concise, bite-sized, and supportive, with no grading language or cold tone.
